raw_case:
Geriatric spontaneous trans-mesenteric herniation leading to death. Case report


Introduction and importance

Spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia is a rare entity in adults. Its pre-operative diagnosis is challenging even with Computed Tomography Scanning. Most cases are diagnosed as incidental findings during laparotomy or postmortem. This case report highlights that even in geriatric patients without prior laparotomies, peritonitis or abdominal trauma which predispose to trans-mesenteric herniation, a differential diagnosis of a spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia is of paramount importance as delay in diagnosis leads to death.

Case presentation

We report a case of a geriatric lady who presented with features of intestinal obstruction. Intra-operative findings revealed strangulated gangrenous small bowels extending from 170 cm from ligament of Treitz to the ileal caecal junction through a trans-mesenteric defect of about 12 cm in diameter. She underwent resection of the gangrenous small bowel with right hemi colectomy and jejunal-transverse colon end to side anastomosis. However, she succumbed two days post operatively.

Clinical discussion

Spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia is a rare cause of small bowel obstruction in adults, difficult to clinically and radiologically diagnose pre-operatively and mostly presents with bowel ischaemia due to strangulation. Early diagnosis is paramount in reducing morbidity and mortality rates in these cases.

Conclusion

Delay in diagnosis of trans-mesenteric hernia leads to strangulation, gangrene of bowels and eventual death. A high index of suspicion is needed from both surgeons and radiologists even in cases of intestinal obstruction without prior histories of laparotomy, peritonitis or abdominal trauma.

Highlights

•Spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia is rare in adults•Delay in management increase the mortality rate.•Trans-mesenteric hernias are often diagnosed intra-operatively or during post-mortems.1

Introduction

Internal hernias can be defined as herniation of intra-peritoneal viscera through peritoneal, mesenteric or omental defects, congenital, acquired or through normal apertures [1] within the peritoneal cavity which accounts to about 0.5–3 % of all causes of intestinal obstruction [2,3]. It carries a high morbidity and mortality rate due to its late diagnosis. Internal hernias can be further subdivided as described by Meyers [1] as para-duodenal hernias 53 %, peri-caecal hernias 13 %, foramen of Winslow hernias 8 %, trans-mesenteric hernias 8 %, hernias into pelvic structures 7 %, trans-meso-sigmoid hernias 6 %, and other types 5 % [1,2].Trans-mesenteric hernia (TMH) is a form of internal hernia through which intra-peritoneal viscera herniate through an anatomic or pathological defect in the mesentery within the peritoneal cavity [4]. It is a rare form of obstruction in adults [4], which upon delay of management may lead to obstruction, strangulation, bowel ischaemia and gangrene with eventual mortality [5]. We report this rare case of a spontaneous trans-mesenteric herniation in a geriatric lady without prior history of laparotomy, peritonitis or abdominal trauma contrary to prior published case settings to highlight its rarity and cement the importance of early diagnosis and management to prevent its morbidity and mortality.2

Methods

This work has been reported in line with the SCARE 2023 criteria [6].3

Case report

A 65-year-old, normotensive, normoglycemic geriatric lady of African descent, housewife, with no formal education status presented to our outpatient department, self-referral from home with progressive four days history of absolute constipation, non-projectile recently eaten food materials vomiting, low grade fever, gradual abdominal distension, sharp umbilical abdominal pain which was fixed, constant and aggravated by feeding attempts with stable vitals. General examination revealed a severe dehydrated, moderate anaemic, cachexic, distressed and ill looking lady. Abdominal examination revealed a uniformly distended abdomen, tender upon palpation, with guarding and reduced bowel sounds on auscultation. The cardiovascular, genitourinary, respiratory, and nervous systems examination were essentially normal.Her supine abdominal x ray revealed multiple dilated loops of small bowel, prominent valvulae conniventes, with multiple air-fluid levels on her erect films were noted (Fig. 1(a) and (b)). A provisional diagnosis of small bowel obstruction possibly due to either intussusception from intra-luminal bowel mass, intra-abdominal extra-luminal bowel mass/tumor obstructing the small bowel or colon mass was made. A spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia or adhesions were not among the pre-operative differential diagnoses made as she had no risk factors such as prior laparotomy, abdominal trauma or peritonitis. She was transferred to the surgical department and scheduled for an emergency laparotomy. Her blood workup revealed haemoglobin level of 12.4 g/dL, with leukocytosis of 17.5 × 109/L. Her HIV and hepatitis panels status were all negative with normal renal (serum urea 6.8 mmol/L and serum creatinine 109 mmol/L) and liver profiles. She was instituted with four liters of Ringers Lactate solution alternating with 5 % dextrose normal saline to rehydrate her. A nasogastric feeding tube was inserted for gastric decompression and an indwelling urethral catheter inserted. She was then wheeled into the operating room for an emergency laparotomy as an ASA III patient. Under general anaesthesia, in supine position, through an extended midline incision, her abdominal cavity was accessed and intra-operatively a gangrenous ischaemic small bowel from 170 cm from Ligament of Treitz to the ileal-caecal junction was noted entangled and strangulated due to herniation into a trans-mesenteric defect 12 cm in diameter. (Fig. 2(a), (b) and (c)). The gangrenous ischaemic bowel was untangled, resected extending from 160 cm from Ligament of Treitz and a right hemicolectomy done with jejunal-transverse colon end to side anastomosis done to re-establish bowel continuity. Only a quarter (160 cm) of the small bowel (jejunum) was left in situ after the entire length of the gangrenous ileum approximately 300 cm was resected. A side to end jejunal-transverse colon anastomosis was then done to re-establish bowel continuity. The abdominal cavity was then irrigated with 10 L of normal saline, to decontaminate the peritoneal cavity and an abdominal drain left in situ. The estimated blood loss was 200mls of blood. The abdominal cavity was then closed in layers with vicryl no 2 closing the abdominal fascia, with interrupted nylon no 2–0 abdominal skin sutures. The procedure was then concluded, and the patient was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) for post-operative care. The procedure was performed by a qualified general surgeon, in a designated district hospital setting, with an average of 150 laparotomies per year. Post-operatively she was continued with mechanical ventilation, given intravenous merogram 1 g twice per 24 h, rehydrated with Ringer's lactate and normal saline solutions 4 l per 24 h and monitored her vitals. Her abdominal drain had about 250mls of serosanguineous fluid on day one and about 200mls of the same fluid day two. No intestinal contents were noted. She unfortunately developed features of generalized septicaemia and her saturation progressively deteriorated and unfortunately succumbed day two postoperatively.4

Timeline

The patient arrived on 14/06/22 at 09:00 am at our outpatient clinic. She was registered in the outpatient clinic register and then directed/wheel-chaired into one of the consultations rooms. At 10:00 am surgical consult was sought, and the patient was transferred to the surgical department. She was received in the department, admitted and preoperative preparations began. At 11:30 am anaesthesia consult reviewed the patient with her laboratory results and cleared her for an emergency laparotomy as ASA III patient. At 02:00 pm she was wheeled into the operating room and cutting time was documented as 02:45 pm. The procedure lasted for 3 h and by 06:00 pm she was in the ICU for post-operative care. On 15/06/22 was her 1st day post operative were IV antibiotics with IV fluids were continued, she was still on mechanical ventilation and drainage tubes contents documented. On 16/06/22 at 02:00 pm the patient succumbed to her condition and was pronounced dead.5

Discussion

Trans-mesenteric hernia (TMH) a form of internal hernias was first described by Rokitansky in 1836 as a postmortem finding [7] and is mostly noted in the paediatric age group [4]. Adult case presentations of TMH are rare [8]. Only thirteen case reports with a male to female ratio of 5:8 have been documented [5]. Trans-mesenteric hernia is seen mostly in cases with histories of prior laparotomies, abdominal trauma or iatrogenic incomplete surgeons' closure of mesenteric defects [4]. This was not the case setting in our patient; an elderly lady who was diagnosed intra-operatively with a spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia with no prior history of laparotomy or abdominal trauma. This is quite rare due to high index potential of herniation of bowels through any defect in the mesentery; thus, late presentations are rare. A similar case scenario was reported by Sang GI KIM et al. [9]. Some cases of TMH are diagnosed intra-operatively as incidental findings in laparotomies of unrelated pathologies [10].A high index of suspicion is needed especially with patients with prior histories of surgeries such as laparoscopies where procedures involving using the Roux loop, or where mesenteric defects are not routinely closed, as the number of trans-mesenteric, trans-mesocolic and retro-anastomotic internal hernias have been on the rise in patients with such histories [1,7].The pathophysiology behind the spontaneous mesenteric defects is thought to be due to prenatal intestinal ischaemia leading to subsequent thinning of the mesenteric leaves. This hypothesis is due to the belief prenatal intestinal ischaemia is also associated with congenital bowel atresia in 5.5 % of the diagnoses [5]. TMH may either be congenital or acquired [3] and may herniate through either a mesenteric or peritoneal defect [5]. Without surgical intervention, with ensuing strangulation they have a mortality rate exceeding 50 % [1,4,7]. Spontaneous resolution is unlikely thus mandating early laparotomy [4,9]. This was evident in our patient as she presented late with the belief of spontaneous resolution of her symptoms and only presented on the fourth day after onset of symptoms. Despite undergoing emergent laparotomy with resection of her gangrenous bowel, she succumbed to features of generalized septicaemia day two post operative.Clinically TMH present in similar fashion as any small bowel obstruction scenario, however more clinically challenging to diagnose [8,11], and often diagnosed intra-operatively or during post-mortem examination [7,12] which maybe challenging to comprehend to unsuspecting and unfamiliar operator [4]. Delay in diagnosis leads to poor prognosis as seen in our case, as the patient presented four days after onset of symptoms. Computed Tomographic scans may aid in the early diagnosis of internal hernias [1,7,10,12], through CT Scan signs of mesenteric ischaemia; the twisting of the mesenteric vessels (the whorl sign) [4].A disproportion in the size of the defect and volume of the herniating bowels leads to strangulation and eventual bowel gangrene [5]. However, even in seemingly large defects as seen in our case (a 12 cm in diameter defect) a larger and longer segment of small bowel herniated approximately 300 cm of bowel loops which lead to almost three quarters of the entire small bowel to turn gangrenous. A large and longer segment of bowel is able to herniate through mesenteric defects due to absence of mesenteric hernia sacs thus despite most mesenteric defects being small a large portion of the small bowel can herniate through a tight opening [4]. This leads to increase in pressure of the herniated bowel, compression of blood vessels by the thickened mesentery leading to early incarceration, ischaemia and gangrene of the entangled bowel [4]. This warrants early diagnosis and management.The diagnosis of small bowel obstruction was easily done in our case clinically and using plain abdominal x rays, however the trans-mesenteric herniation with the strangulated gangrenous bowels was diagnosed intra-operatively. Such cases pose a clinical and radiological challenge preoperatively and need a high index of suspicion from the attending clinician.6

Conclusion

Delay in diagnosis of trans-mesenteric hernia leads to strangulation, gangrene of bowels and eventual death. A high index of suspicion is needed from both surgeons and radiologists even in cases of intestinal obstruction without prior histories of laparotomy, peritonitis or abdominal trauma.

Consent for publication

Written informed consent was obtained from the patients family for publication for this case report.

Ethical approval

Ethical approval for this study; case report (Ethical Committee number MHM/GS/CR/02/24) was provided by the Ethical Committee Military Hospital Mwanza, Department of General Surgery, United Republic of Tanzania, on 09 August 2024.

Funding

No funding was obtained for this case report.

Guarantor

Ahmed Ali Shabhay.

CRediT authorship contribution statement


Ahmed Shabhay - lead surgeon, conceptualization and writing of the script.Zarina Shabhay - reviewed and edited the script.Fabian Anaclet Massaga - reviewed and edited the script.Amri Salim Mwami - reviewed and edited the script/anaesthesiologist.David Msuya - reviewed and edited the script.Kondo Chilonga - reviewed and edited the script.


Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare they have no competing interests. All authors of the manuscript have read and agreed to its contents.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 65-year-old African female, housewife, no prior surgical history.
- Chief Complaint: Four-day history of absolute constipation, vomiting, and abdominal pain.
- History of Present Illness: Progressive symptoms of intestinal obstruction including vomiting, fever, and fixed umbilical abdominal pain aggravated by food intake.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: No prior surgeries or risk factors for mesenteric defects.
- Family History: Not indicated.
- Physical Examination: Severely dehydrated, moderate anemia, cachexic, distressed; abdominal distension, tenderness, guarding, and reduced bowel sounds.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Supine abdominal X-ray: Dilated small bowel loops and air-fluid levels.
  - Blood work: Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, leukocytosis (17.5 × 109/L), normal renal and liver profiles; HIV and hepatitis panels negative.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:** Based on abdominal obstruction, initial differential diagnoses included:
   - Intussusception from intra-luminal bowel mass.
   - Intra-abdominal extra-luminal mass.
   - Obstruction from a colon mass.

2. **Exclusion of Common Causes:**
   - History of absence of laparotomy, abdominal trauma, or peritonitis reduced the likelihood of adhesions or surgical complications as etiology.

3. **Surgical Finding:** During emergency laparotomy, trans-mesenteric hernia with strangulated gangrenous bowels was identified and resected. Diagnosis confirmed intra-operatively.

4. **Rationale for Final Diagnosis:** Clinical progression, imaging, and intra-operative findings revealed bowel obstruction due to spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia—a rare condition with significant risks of ischemia and gangrene due to vascular compromise.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Spontaneous trans-mesenteric hernia with strangulated gangrenous small bowel resulting in bowel ischemia and gangrene.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- Patient Information: 65-year-old African female, housewife, no prior surgical history.
- Chief Complaint: Four-day history of absolute constipation, vomiting, and abdominal pain.
- History of Present Illness: Progressive symptoms of intestinal obstruction including vomiting, fever, and fixed umbilical abdominal pain aggravated by food intake.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: No prior surgeries or risk factors for mesenteric defects.
- Family History: Not indicated.
- Physical Examination: Severely dehydrated, moderate anemia, cachexic, distressed; abdominal distension, tenderness, guarding, and reduced bowel sounds.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Supine abdominal X-ray: Dilated small bowel loops and air-fluid levels.
  - Blood work: Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, leukocytosis (17.5 × 109/L), normal renal and liver profiles; HIV and hepatitis panels negative.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's symptoms. She's a 65-year-old African female with a four-day history of absolute constipation, vomiting, and abdominal pain. The chief complaint includes intestinal obstruction symptoms like vomiting, fever, and fixed umbilical pain that gets worse with eating. 

First, the physical exam shows she's severely dehydrated, moderately anemic, and cachexic. The abdomen is distended, tender, with guarding and reduced bowel sounds. These signs point towards a possible bowel obstruction. The abdominal X-ray shows dilated small bowel loops and air-fluid levels, which are classic for intestinal obstruction.

Her blood work shows leukocytosis (high white blood cell count) which suggests infection or inflammation. The hemoglobin is 12.4, which is low but not extremely so, and the other labs like renal and liver profiles are normal. HIV and hepatitis are negative, so no chronic infections to consider here.

Now, considering the presentation: sudden onset of obstruction with vomiting, abdominal pain, and distension. The fact that it's fixed umbilical pain might suggest a mechanical obstruction. The lack of prior surgery or risk factors for mesenteric defects makes adhesions less likely. But what else could cause this?

In elderly patients, especially women, one has to consider malignancies. Bowel cancer can cause mechanical obstruction. Also, diverticulitis might be a possibility, but she doesn't have a history of that. However, her age and presentation could still point towards a tumor. 

Another thought is volvulus, which is twisting of the bowel. That can cause acute obstruction and is more common in certain populations. But without a history of prior surgery or risk factors, maybe less likely. However, in some regions, volvulus is more common.

But given the age, the presence of a mass or tumor is a concern. The physical exam mentions guarding, which could indicate peritonitis, suggesting a perforation. But the X-ray doesn't show free air, so maybe not a perforation yet. However, the leukocytosis could be due to infection from obstruction leading to bowel ischemia or perforation.

Alternatively, a fecal impaction could cause obstruction, but that's usually more in the colon, and the X-ray shows small bowel dilation. So maybe high small bowel obstruction. 

Considering the lack of prior surgery, but the presence of a fixed pain, and the age, the most likely diagnosis is a mechanical obstruction due to a tumor, possibly in the small bowel. However, other differentials include adhesions, but she has no prior surgery. 

Wait, but the question says no prior surgical history. So adhesions are less likely. Then, the next options are tumors, volvulus, or strictures from other causes. In an elderly female, cancer is a significant concern. 

So putting it all together: the findings are consistent with intestinal obstruction, likely mechanical. Given her age and the absence of other risk factors, the most probable cause is a malignancy causing the obstruction, such as small bowel adenocarcinoma or a metastatic lesion. Alternatively, a volvulus could be possible, but without specific risk factors. However, the presence of leukocytosis might indicate an inflammatory component, but without infection markers, it's unclear. 

Another angle: the physical exam mentions cachexia, which can be associated with malignancy. Also, the anemia could be due to chronic blood loss from a tumor. 

So the final diagnosis would be intestinal obstruction due to a mechanical cause, most likely from a tumor. However, the exact type would need further imaging or endoscopy. But based on the given data, the main diagnosis is intestinal obstruction, probably from a tumor.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a four-day history of absolute constipation, vomiting, and abdominal pain, which are classic symptoms of intestinal obstruction. The progressive nature of symptoms, including fever and fixed umbilical pain exacerbated by food, suggests a mechanical obstruction rather than a functional one.  
<step 2> Physical examination reveals signs consistent with bowel obstruction: abdominal distension, tenderness, guarding, and reduced bowel sounds. Severe dehydration and moderate anemia further support a prolonged obstruction leading to fluid loss and potential chronic blood loss.  
<step 3> The supine abdominal X-ray findings of dilated small bowel loops and air-fluid levels confirm the diagnosis of intestinal obstruction. The absence of free air rules out perforation, but the presence of leukocytosis (17.5 × 10⁹/L) indicates inflammation or infection, possibly secondary to ischemia or partial obstruction.  
<step 4> Given the patient’s age (65 years), lack of prior surgery, and absence of risk factors for adhesions, the most likely cause of mechanical obstruction is a neoplasm (e.g., small bowel adenocarcinoma, metastatic lesion, or lymphoma). Cachexia and anemia may reflect chronic disease or occult bleeding.  
<step 5> While volvulus or strictures from inflammatory bowel disease are alternatives, the patient’s demographic profile and clinical findings (e.g., no prior risk factors, no chronic inflammatory history) make malignancy the most probable underlying etiology.  

### Answer:
Intestinal obstruction secondary to small bowel malignancy.  
```
